acceptance_criteria: Support Cockpit is documented as the selected prototype direction in pack-local state and specs.
The Starlight publishing path begins moving toward the Support Cockpit interaction model without changing the JSON-first artifact contract.
The next implementation cycle preserves the sticky quick-jump behavior, collapsible checkpoints, and command-first presentation.
completion_signals: Pack-local objective, backlog, work-state, and readiness files all reflect Support Cockpit as the chosen direction.
The remote Codex runtime is restaged with the updated build-pack state and given a concrete Support Cockpit implementation task.
Remote Codex invocation challenges and mitigations are documented in a pack-local note so the next implementation cycle starts from a cleaner launcher pattern.
escalation_conditions: The Support Cockpit direction starts to require account state, saved case state, or broader product platform work.
The next publishing iteration would replace the machine-readable baseline model instead of rendering it.
tasks.summary: Adopt Support Cockpit as the selected Starlight publishing direction and drive the next implementation cycle through that chassis.

acceptance_criteria: The build-pack defines a canonical artifact model for runtime evidence, service inventory, and baseline output.
The first artifact model clearly separates observed facts, inference, and unknowns.
completion_signals: The CLI and source layout expose named surfaces for the first ADF artifact set.
The pack-local objective, backlog, and work-state files all point at the same first implementation slice.
escalation_conditions: The artifact model cannot stay small enough for the MVP without reopening product scope.
The required output set starts to imply broad test or fixture expansion.
tasks.summary: Define the first bounded ADF artifact model for runtime evidence, service inventory, and baseline output.

acceptance_criteria: The build-pack can collect a safe first-pass AlgoSec runtime snapshot from the lab appliance.
The first collection path stays read-only and bounded to support-relevant evidence classes.
completion_signals: A runtime collection command exists for the first ADF slice.
The collection output includes service inventory and core platform checkpoints.
escalation_conditions: The target appliance requires risky write actions or unsupported privilege changes to gather the first evidence set.
The first-pass evidence set expands beyond the MVP boundary of useful support diagnostics.
tasks.summary: Implement the first safe runtime evidence collection path for the controlled AlgoSec lab appliance.

acceptance_criteria: The build-pack renders machine-readable baseline artifacts into a simple support-facing HTML view.
The HTML output remains a render of canonical JSON artifacts rather than a separate source of truth.
completion_signals: The first HTML-ready baseline output exists for the AlgoSec lab path.
Support-facing output is generated from the same artifact model used for machine-readable evidence.
escalation_conditions: The HTML layer starts to dominate the scope instead of remaining a thin render of machine-readable output.
The support-facing output requires broad UX work beyond a simple MVP render.
tasks.summary: Generate the first HTML-ready support output from canonical ADF baseline artifacts.

acceptance_criteria: The build-pack can generate Astro Starlight site source from the canonical ADF baseline without replacing JSON as the source of truth.
The Rocky lab appliance can install and build the generated Astro Starlight site successfully with the system Node runtime.
The publishing direction stays portable so the long-term rendered site can later move to a separate Ubuntu host.
completion_signals: The build-pack exposes a generate-starlight-site command that writes package.json, Astro config, content collection files, markdown playbook pages, and renderer assets from support-baseline.json.
The lab appliance has a successful Astro Starlight build under the generated starlight-site artifact root.
escalation_conditions: The publishing layer starts to compete with the runtime evidence model instead of rendering it.
The Starlight path requires deep platform or hosting work before the support content is proven.
tasks.summary: Prototype Astro Starlight publishing from the canonical ADF baseline and verify the site can build on the Rocky lab appliance.

acceptance_criteria: The top support domains use language and sequencing that match how junior support engineers actually diagnose customer issues from an SSH session.
The highest-value decision playbooks are tuned for operator review without changing the canonical JSON-first model.
Priority playbooks use the current Check, Run, Verification, Linux note, and Known working example pattern.
The wording stays plain, short, and easy to follow for engineers using English as a second language.
completion_signals: ASMS UI is down reflects the reviewed SSH-first four-step Linux-first flow inside the cleaned Support Cockpit chassis, and FireFlow Backend follows next.
HTML and JSON outputs remain aligned after the language tuning pass.
escalation_conditions: Requested wording changes imply a broader UX rewrite instead of playbook tuning.
Support guidance starts to require risky write actions instead of read-only diagnostics.
tasks.summary: Continue tuning high-value support playbooks into final SSH-first operator language and decision flow, using the Support Cockpit chassis as the next operator-facing container.

acceptance_criteria: Every major playbook can start from a shared host health pre-check pack before moving into subsystem-specific checks.
Priority playbooks embed service-status, listener, config, and log commands for more AlgoSec services and dependencies.
Each command remains read-only and includes a healthy signal plus failure interpretation.
Priority commands include short Linux note blocks, copy controls, and known working terminal examples where the target appliance can provide them.
Rare path or config checks stay deferred unless a playbook truly needs them.
completion_signals: A shared host health pre-check pack exists for disk, memory, OOM, and related host-resource checks used across major playbooks.
The command bundles extend beyond the current top-path checks and cover more service-specific failure paths.
Service ports, config checkpoints, and known working command output are surfaced more explicitly in the operator-facing output.
The ASMS UI path proves a reusable service-pack pattern with lab-backed status, listener, functional, and file-log checks for Keycloak and ms-metro.
escalation_conditions: The required command coverage implies broad appliance-specific scripting beyond the MVP boundary.
The next command set would require unsupported or risky write access on the target appliance.
tasks.summary: Expand service-specific diagnostic command coverage for priority services and dependencies, starting from the reusable ASMS UI service-pack pattern.

acceptance_criteria: A fixed shared host health pre-check pack is now defined for every major playbook before subsystem-specific checks.
completion_signals: A fixed shared host health pre-check pack is now defined for every major playbook before subsystem-specific checks.

Disk, memory, OOM, and other host-resource checks are foundational Linux triage steps in every scenario. Defining them as a standard reusable pre-check keeps playbooks consistent and helps engineers start in the same place every time.
escalation_conditions: Stop and escalate if this task would require widening ADF scope, unsupported PackFactory mutations, or unreviewed risk.
tasks.summary: A fixed shared host health pre-check pack is now defined for every major playbook before subsystem-specific checks.

acceptance_criteria: Use one real authenticated GUI flow plus same-minute log correlation to prove that the ASMS UI playbook can be refined from observed runtime behavior instead of guessed service order.
completion_signals: Use one real authenticated GUI flow plus same-minute log correlation to prove that the ASMS UI playbook can be refined from observed runtime behavior instead of guessed service order.

This is the first strong proof that ADF's systems-thinking method reduces support ambiguity in a real GUI-down path. It shows that a bounded browser flow, module-aware log correlation, and careful observed-versus-inferred wording can produce a more trustworthy troubleshooting model than a service-first page.
escalation_conditions: Stop and escalate if this task would require widening ADF scope, unsupported PackFactory mutations, or unreviewed risk.
tasks.summary: Use one real authenticated GUI flow plus same-minute log correlation to prove that the ASMS UI playbook can be refined from observed runtime behavior instead of guessed service order.

acceptance_criteria: Use the reproduced post-home browser minute to keep immediate home-shell follow-ups inside the ASMS playbook and defer later subsystem-looking routes until the evidence says they are the true first stop point.
completion_signals: Use the reproduced post-home browser minute to keep immediate home-shell follow-ups inside the ASMS playbook and defer later subsystem-looking routes until the evidence says they are the true first stop point.
This lowers troubleshooting effort after login succeeds. It keeps the engineer on the shortest next checks instead of jumping too early into every nearby subsystem and background route.
escalation_conditions: Stop and escalate if this task would require widening ADF scope, unsupported PackFactory mutations, or unreviewed risk.
tasks.summary: Use the reproduced post-home browser minute to keep immediate home-shell follow-ups inside the ASMS playbook and defer later subsystem-looking routes until the evidence says they are the true first stop point.

completion_signals: Use the authenticated lab evidence to treat BusinessFlow as the first named operational checkpoint in the ASMS path and FireFlow as a later auth-coupled module with explicit support-useful checks.

This turns the ASMS UI model into a lower-effort troubleshooting path for support engineers. It gives the engineer a stronger named checkpoint than a generic Keycloak-first guess and keeps FireFlow present only where the observed login journey says it matters.

completion_signals: Investigate each major ASMS UI dependency component more thoroughly so the rebuilt playbook uses stronger useful-work checks instead of mostly restructured status and grep commands.

completion_signals: Keep the current shared host pre-check commands, but reframe them so they reveal the pressure signal being checked and help the engineer reach a quick current assessment of the system.

ADF is now optimizing for engineer effort during live support. The host pre-check should not only show raw resource values. It should help the engineer see disk pressure, inode pressure, memory pressure, CPU pressure, and similar system conditions quickly and consistently.

tasks.summary: Keep the current shared host pre-check commands, but reframe them so they reveal the pressure signal being checked and help the engineer reach a quick current assessment of the system.

completion_signals: Consider whether ADF should gradually evolve from a documentation-style playbook surface into a small scenario-based clickable dashboard for support engineers.

ADF is starting to feel like a support engineer's support engineer. A bounded dashboard model could help engineers choose a likely starting scenario quickly and then work through collapsible diagnostic paths with less friction during live cases.

acceptance_criteria: Build a bounded dependency map for the ASMS UI subsystem from lab evidence, then validate a small set of common failure scenarios against that map.
completion_signals: Build a bounded dependency map for the ASMS UI subsystem from lab evidence, then validate a small set of common failure scenarios against that map.

The ASMS UI path likely depends on a small set of important services such as httpd, keycloak, ms-metro, and possibly activemq, but the relationship needs to be mapped from evidence instead of guessed. A bounded map plus a few high-value scenarios will make the playbook far more trustworthy than shallow service checks or unlimited speculative branching.
escalation_conditions: Stop and escalate if this task would require widening ADF scope, unsupported PackFactory mutations, or unreviewed risk.
tasks.summary: Build a bounded dependency map for the ASMS UI subsystem from lab evidence, then validate a small set of common failure scenarios against that map.

acceptance_criteria: Record a plain working note that explains why ADF does not need continuous full synchronization between Root, the ADF Build Pack, and ADF Remote while ADF Remote remains reachable and is accepted as the active working copy.
Separate Root-owned autonomy and memory infrastructure from ADF Remote-owned content truth, and identify the mixed control-plane files that need selective reconciliation instead of blind copying.
Define checkpoint moments for synchronization such as accepted remote progress, promotion/readiness review, risky remote experimentation, or reusable logic pullback.
Record when Git or another source-control workflow would reduce overhead better than PackFactory-mediated mirroring.
completion_signals: A saved working document exists under `eval/history/` and explains the checkpointed-sync model in plain language.
The document gives a file-by-file reconciliation bucket for `carry from Root`, `carry from ADF Remote`, and `reconcile carefully`.
A later reviewer can tell why PackFactory should not be used as a minute-by-minute Git substitute for the live ADF Remote workspace.
escalation_conditions: Stop and escalate if the proposed synchronization model would require unsupported PackFactory control-plane changes or hidden assumptions about the remote host persistence model.
tasks.summary: Define the ADF synchronization model: keep Root as the autonomy/control-plane authority, treat ADF Remote as the active content workspace, and use checkpointed pullback instead of continuous mirroring.
validation_commands: rg -q '^# ADF Root Build Pack Remote Reconciliation Plan$' eval/history/adf-root-build-pack-remote-reconciliation-plan-20260326.md

acceptance_criteria: ADF tracks one explicit launcher-stability task for remote Codex work on the lab appliance instead of leaving the issue scattered across notes and ad hoc terminal recovery.
The task defines a preferred launcher pattern that consistently bypasses the appliance interactive menu for both short control runs and longer investigation loops.
completion_signals: A single supported remote launcher pattern is documented and used consistently for prompt, launcher, result, stderr, and log-file handling.
Remote Codex can be relaunched cleanly after interrupted or stale runs without falling back to trial-and-error shell quoting.
The ADF pack-local notes and state no longer rely on multiple improvised menu-bypass variants to describe a successful remote run.
escalation_conditions: The launcher fix would require changing appliance login policy or unsupported OS-level shell behavior outside the controlled lab boundaries.
The standardization effort starts to sprawl into broad factory-wide remote-runtime redesign before the ADF pack proves its bounded launcher path.
tasks.summary: Standardize one remote Codex launcher path that consistently bypasses the appliance interactive menu and shell-wrapper drift for both short control runs and longer ADF investigation loops.

Design how ADF Codex work can run on a separate Ubuntu 24.04 LTS control host that connects over the network to a Rocky-based target application lab instead of coupling the agent runtime to the target application server itself.
acceptance_criteria: ADF records one explicit design task for decoupling the Codex runtime host from the target application lab environment instead of assuming the agent must run on the same server as the target application.
The task identifies the preferred communication process, protocol, and bounded specification between an Ubuntu 24.04 LTS Codex host and the Rocky-based target application environment.
The task defines how PackFactory remote autonomy and memory-loop workflows should operate when the build-pack runtime host and the target application host are different systems.
escalation_conditions: Stop and escalate if the design would require unsupported direct network control over the Rocky application host, unclear security boundaries, or broad PackFactory-wide remote-runtime redesign before the ADF pack proves the bounded pattern first.

Stop and escalate if the runtime split cannot be specified without first defining a clear ownership model for credentials, execution paths, and evidence transport between the Ubuntu control host and the Rocky target host.
completion_signals: ADF has a named plan artifact that explains how a separate Ubuntu Codex host should communicate with the Rocky application lab, what protocol and process it should use, and how PackFactory remote autonomy workflows map onto that split-host model.
A later implementation pass can tell whether the build-pack should remain local, remote-managed, or split-host remote-managed without guessing about source of truth or transport behavior.
